headlines.title: Judge Hunter Nails Some Campaign Misrepresentations
headlines.fullText:
Comanche, Texas, May 27, 1914.
To the Public:
Mr. Oscar Callaway, in his speeches at Weatherford and at Arlington, and, I suppose, at Thorpe Springs and other places, made the emphatic statement that I am "drawing a pension of 824.00 per month," and I have heard that Mayor Milam is whis- pering the same around quietly in his gum-shoe campaign against me in our race for Congress. I don't consider that drawing a pension from the state or nation would be any disgrace or serious objection to any candidate for Congress. I rather consider it a mark of honor. I, however, denounce the statement as absolutely false. A certificate from the Commissioner of Pensions at Wash- ington would settle the question, and if they will produce such I will quit the race. I hope they will be gentlemen enough to withdraw such statements as publicly as they have made them, or at once produce the certificate of the Pension Commissioner. I can prove every statement I have printed in my reasons for opposing the re-election of Mr. Callaway and more, and will do 50. Mr. Bryan's certificate only states that "his attention had never been called to anything in the record attributed to you (Cal- laway) . . . but if you (Callaway) ever said anything un- friendly of me (Bryan) I do not know it." Of course not, nor did the lion know that the gnat was stinging him on his mane. Mr. Callaway spoke his sarcastic, invective speech against Mr. Bryan during Taft's administration, and among his Republican friends and allies, with whom he stood and voted, and who applauded him vociferously when he made his little speech against the great- est man in the world. These great Republican leaders applauded his treachery to the Democratic leader of our progressive party, but no doubt despised the traitor.
Ever since April 28, last, I have been publishing an "open letter" to Mayor Milam in nearly all the papers in this district, wherein I ask him if he is a prohibitionist or an antiprohibition- ist? Whether he made speeches and voted for Jake Wolters, the attorney and leader of the liquor interests of Texas, against Mor- ris Sheppard for United States Senator? And whether if elected to Congress he will help Morris Sheppard get us nationwide prohibition? And Mr. Milam has never answered this open let- ter yet. I now state to the people whose vote he is seeking that he is an antiprohibitionist ; campaigned and voted against sub- mission ; campaigned and voted for Jake Wolters.
Tarrant county has issued over 21,000 poll tax receipts and certificates this year and the prohibitionists are determined to carry the county this year and will vote their full strength, and I will carry this prohibition vote solid against the liquor mayor of Fort Worth, and beat him two to one among the antis, except the liquor dealers.
In 1912 I beat two opponents for the legislature-one a pro and the other an anti-got more votes than both of them put to- gether in Tarrant county, and they were both clean, honorable, truthful, popular men, and I never left my desk, and I know I can do it again against my present opponents.
I have lived 30 years among those people, and we have fought shoulder to shoulder together for Jim Hogg, William J. Bryan and Woodrow Wilson and always won. I have lived in Texas 41 years and have alway's been a Democrat and never scratched the ticket, not even when Parker ran for President.
The first Winchester rifle I ever saw I bought and went to Austin with a band of Confederate veterans to unseat E. J. Davis and seat Richard Coke as Governor of Texas, and I have been fighting for the people ever since. I never asked my people for a political office before this race, and I want them to support me now, for I am going to Congress to do something for that class of men whom Congress has forgotten for fifty years. I know how to do it and will do it. And I am going there, too, in spite of the campaign stories. Your friend for the people,
SAM J. HUNTER.

headlines.title: First Arrest in War on Peddlers' Shouting Is Made
headlines.fullText:
"Black burrees! Blackburrees-two for fifteen cents! Blackburrees I got got black burrees.
A. Sletel and Sam Shuchvercar, Italian peddlers, living at 1208 West Seventh street, were winding up the chorus to this shouting tune at First and Jones streets when Officer Ben Dollins arrested them. Disturbing the peste was the charge The Itallans appeared in police court Thursday morning and Judge Bardin fined them Dollins testified that the Italians wed head Tele selling irritate ed the condition of a sick woman at First and Jones streets, he said. As the peddlers refused to heed the wars- ing of the woman's husband, Dollins went to the corner and laid for them. These are the first arrests in a war which the police recently started against noisy peddlers. They have got to modify their selling-voices or go to jail, according to police warning.

headlines.title: Judge Hunter Proves Pension Certificate Error
headlines.fullText:
Comanche, Texas, May 29, 1914.
To the People of Texas:
Mr. Oscar Callaway, ever since his return from Washington, about three or four weeks ago, in his campaign speeches against me, has told his audiences and has published in the newspapers that "Judge Sam J. Hunter of Fort Worth, Texas, is a wealthy. able-bodied lawyer and is drawing a pension from the United States government of 824 a month." During all this time I sup- pose he had in his possession a certificate purporting to have been issued by the pension office at Washington certifying said facts. He never published the certificate itself, or showed it to me or any of my friends, so far as I ever heard. I met him on the side- walk in Comanche Wednesday afternoon and denounced his statement as untrue, when he drew from his pocket a certificate from the Pension office containing the statement that the pen- sion office records showed that "Sam J. Hunter of Fort Worth, Texas, was a member of the 167th Illinois Volunteers, in our late Civil War, in Company F, and was now drawing a pension of $24 per month." I read it and told him that it did not refer to me. Believing it to be an honest document, I told him there must be some other Sam Hunter in Fort Worth who was in fact drawing a pension, and phoned my son at Fort Worth to find out and wire me. This was early Thursday morning. Later Thursday my son phoned me that there was no Sam J. Hunter in Fort Worth on the books of the Pension Agent of that city, but that he had wired the Commissioner of Pensions at Washington to wire him the facts. On the following morning I received the fol- lowing original telegram which the Commissioner of Pensions had wired my son:
Mackay Telegraph-Cable Company. Connecting with POSTAL TELEGRAPH-COMMERCIAL CABLES. TELEGRAM Clarence H. Mackay, President.
Received at Delivery No. 504. The Mackay Telegraph-Cable Company transmits and delivers this message subject to the terms and conditions printed on the back of this blank.
28 Ks. I. 27Collect 3:22pm 1 extra
WASHINGTON, D. C., May 28, 1914. Ray Hunter, Fort Worth, Texas. Only one Sam J. Hunter on pension rolls. His address is Irving, Illinois. No Sam J. Hunter of Fort Worth, Texas, is a United States pensioner.
G. M. Saltzgaber, Comm'r.
Mayor Milam, as I have been creditably informed, has been whispering around among the people substantially the same state- ment, and that I am a Republican. Do the honorable people of this district and state desire such timber to represent them in Congress? I leave the question for you to answer by your Demo- cratic votes on July 25, 1914. PROHIBITIONISTS, STAND BY YOUR GUNS AND THE VICTORY IS OURS!
I have written the Commissioner to send me his official cer- tificate confirming the above telegram, and I will publish it when received. Faithfully yours, SAM J. HUNTER.

headlines.title: SCORES MILAM.
headlines.fullText:
To the Fort Worth Record:
At Home, May 31, 1914.
I want my people at home to know why I drew the little vest-pocket pistol on Mr. Callaway when he struck me in the face on the sidewalk at Comanche last Wednesday.
I had circulars printed at Comanche denouncing as untrue the statement of Mr. Callaway that I was drawing a pension of $24 per month. I was about to distribute and had distributed some of these circulars, when a friend who had read one told me that Mr. Callaway was very likely to attack me on the street, if he should meet me distributing them; that he was a powerful man, physically, and could jerk me down and beat me, and ad- vised me to send them around by boys. I did send some by a boy and carried some myself in order to meet the people and get a personal acquaintance, but I went first to my hotel and took from my traveling bag the little Colt automatic pistol and placed it in my coat pocket, with the intention of scaring him off of me if he should attempt to attack me with violence. I never had a thought of killing him or hurting him, but I knew that I was not able to cope with him in a personal encounter, but I did not want him to beat me up for defending my honor and my good name. We met in a crowd which had gathered around him and to them he was detailing the pension statement, which I at once to his face denounced as untrue, when he handed me a certificate pur- porting to come from the pension commissioner at Washington. I took it and read it carefully and saw that it said "Sam J. Hunter of Fort Worth, Texas, was a member of the 167th Illinois Volun- teers, Company F, and was drawing 824 pension per month from the United States government." I said to him and the crowd: "This certificate does not refer to me, but there must be some other Sam J. Hunter in Fort Worth, and I assured him I was not a pensioner for any amount whatever. He said some ugly things to me and we disputed with each other for a little while when his language became very insulting, and seeing he was try- ing to provoke me into a difficulty I pushed my way through the crowd past him, saying at the same time I was not a pensioner and that his statement was false, when he turned half around and told me I was a liar. I then promptly responded that he was a - liar, when he struck me a stunning back-handed lick in the face, causing my mouth and nose to bleed considerably. While stinging, under the pain of this blow I drew my pistol. released the safety and sighted it on his heart, expecting him to leap on me and beat me, and if he had I would have pulled the trigger probably more than once, but seeing I had stood him off I was about to replace my pistol in my pocket and go away. when some powerful man seized my hand and pistol from behind me and jerked my arm backward so violently as to break a liga- ment in my fore shoulder and I suppose caused me to faint, for I knew nothing more until I awoke to consciousness lying seven of eight feet from the sidewalk in the mud of the street with Mr. Callaway on me, while his friend was holding my right hand and lying on it and twisting the pistol out of my hand. I heard him say: "Get off of him, Callaway, I have his gun." It is hardly necessary to impart the information to my friends who know me, that if I could have used my hand and arm then while Mr. Callaway was on me, and had had the pistol, I would have killed him.
I have lived in Fort Worth 30 years and never had a diffi- culty with any man, and I have been a fighter at the bar and in politics for my friends, and I have rarely carried a pistol on my person. It is only when I have cause to believe that I am in dan- ger of personal violence that I have carried a pistol in my pocket.
I wish now to say that I have learned from the Commissioner of Pensions that his certificate about me is an error and I have requested the Commissioner to investigate the matter.
The personal part of the difficulty has passed with me if he will keep away from me.
I hope my friends will see from this that I was justifiable in putting the pistol in my pocket for defense of my person. SAM J. HUNTER.

headlines.title: SOUTH SIDE HOME IS ENTERED BY BURGLARS
headlines.fullText:
The home of Roy Binyon, 1405 Wash- ington avenue, was burglarized Wed- nesday night while the family was asleep. The burglar unlatched a rear screen door and made his way to Bin- yon's bedroom, found a pair of trous. ers and rifled the pockets-taking a gold watch, $3.80 in change and a gold locket.
The Binyons knew nothing about it until they awoke Thursday morning The prowler left no clew.

headlines.title: Civil War Correspondent Dies.
headlines.fullText: CHICAGO, June 4 .- George Hosmer. former war correspondent and editorial writer, is dead at Summit, N. J, aged acted as a correspondent for the New York Herald.

Whatever other mediums advertisers may use-they must in the last analysis use the newspapers directly or indirectly. Many wise national advertisers now use the daily newspapers in large volume, because through them they not only reach the people but also inspire confidence in the distributers of their goods. National advertisers interested in ways of making their advertising more productive are invited to address the Bureau of Advertising, American Newspaper Publishers Association, 806 World Building, New York.
